examples.title: Basic Command Line Arguments Display
examples.description: Program that displays all command line arguments passed to it
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates how argc and argv work. The loop prints each argument with its index. argv[argc] is shown to be NULL. Run as: ./program one two three

examples.title: Sum of Two Numbers from Command Line
examples.description: Accepts two integers as arguments and prints their sum
examples.explanation: The program first checks argc to ensure exactly 3 arguments (program + 2 numbers). Then uses atoi() to convert string arguments to integers and calculates the sum. Run as: ./add 10 20

examples.title: Calculator with Command Line Arguments
examples.description: Performs arithmetic operations based on operator argument
examples.explanation: This calculator program accepts three arguments: two numbers and an operator. It validates input count, converts strings to integers, performs the operation using switch-case, and handles division by zero. Run as: ./calc 10 + 5

examples.title: Python Equivalent of Command Line Args
examples.description: Python program demonstrating argv equivalent using sys module
examples.code:
import sys

def main():
    # sys.argv is similar to argv in C
    # sys.argv[0] = script name
    # sys.argv[1:] = actual arguments
    
    argc = len(sys.argv)
    
    print(f"Total arguments (argc): {argc}")
    print("Arguments passed:")
    
    for i in range(argc):
        print(f"argv[{i}] = {sys.argv[i]}")
    
    # Converting string to int (equivalent to atoi)
    if argc >= 2:
        num = int(sys.argv[1])
        print(f"Converted number: {num}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: Python's sys.argv works similarly to C's argv. sys.argv[0] is the script name, and sys.argv[1:] are user arguments. Use int() or float() for conversion (similar to atoi/atof). Run as: python script.py 42
